extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system exhibits a **decentralized yet hierarchical architecture** with **five physical containers** (representing the crane’s components) and **six digital twin containers** (including the Physical Crane, Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, and potentially additional services). The scope of responsibilities is modular: the **Physical Crane container** manages real-time actuation and sensor data, while the **Database container** stores state information. The **Dashboard container** provides runtime visualization, and the **Trajectory Generator container** handles path planning for puck movements. Coordination mechanisms are not explicitly detailed, but the system’s CPS nature suggests **event-driven or publish-subscribe patterns** (e.g., via message brokers) for inter-container communication. The architecture avoids a monolithic design, enabling **scalable twinning** for multiple crane instances if extended, though the documents do not specify distributed synchronization protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation for the lab-scale gantry crane follows an **ad-hoc, containerized architecture** without reliance on a commercial digital twin framework. The system is orchestrated using the **C4 Model**, which defines five **physical containers** (e.g., crane hardware, sensors) and six **digital twin containers** (e.g., Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator). Component integration is achieved through **modular containerization**, likely leveraging lightweight virtualization or container platforms (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) for deployment. Resource management is implicit in the containerized design, with each container handling distinct responsibilities (e.g., real-time control vs. visualization). System-wide coordination is implied to be **container-centric**, with dependencies between containers (e.g., Trajectory Generator → Physical Crane) ensuring synchronized operation. The absence of a commercial framework suggests custom integration logic, potentially using **message-passing or shared databases** (e.g., InfluxDB-like systems) for state synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with external systems via **MQTT-based publish-subscribe communication** for real-time signaling between physical and digital components. The **Physical Crane Interface** (implemented in Python) serves as a critical integration layer, translating low-level sensor/actuator data into higher-level digital twin representations. While no explicit enterprise interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) are mentioned, the use of **MQTT** ensures lightweight, scalable data exchange. Dependencies on external systems are minimal, with the primary focus on internal coordination via the MQTT Broker. The system does not explicitly detail integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems beyond its own containerized architecture.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin for the lab-scale gantry crane incorporates **CAD models** for geometric representation and a **kinematic model** for behavioral simulation of the crane's motion dynamics. Data management is handled via a **TimescaleDB database**, which stores time-series operational data. The system includes a **Simulation Service** that executes predictive and behavioral models, enabling real-time comparison with physical measurements. The Digital Twin constellation integrates these models through a structured architecture, where the CAD and kinematic models serve as foundational representations, while the Simulation Service dynamically validates and refines predictions using real-time data from the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components of the Digital Twin system include **Motors and associated Controllers**, specifically a **Cart Motor [Component: Stepper]** for horizontal movement and an **Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist for container manipulation. The system leverages these actuators to execute predefined trajectories, with no explicit mention of remote control interfaces, actuation ranges, or safety constraints in the provided documents. The design is tailored for a lab-scale gantry crane system inspired by harbor cranes, where precise control of container movement is required. No commercial or proprietary actuation frameworks are referenced; the implementation appears ad-hoc, relying on direct hardware integration with the Digital Twin's simulation and validation services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The physical sensing infrastructure includes a **Swing Angle Encoder** to measure the swinging motion of the hoist and an **Anemometer** to measure wind speed. The spatial distribution of sensors is not explicitly detailed, but their roles are specialized: the Swing Angle Encoder monitors dynamic behavior during container transport, while the Anemometer provides environmental context for operational conditions. No specifications regarding sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or communication protocols (e.g., serial, CAN bus) are provided in the documents. Data collection mechanisms are implied to interface directly with the Digital Twin's data management layer, though no explicit protocols or technologies (e.g., Raspberry Pi, InfluxDB) are mentioned for this specific system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical Crane Interface** acts as the primary data acquisition layer, interfacing directly with the crane's controllers via a Python program to collect real-time operational data. This data is transmitted to the **Logging Application**, a Python-based service that continuously records the crane’s state (e.g., positional data, trajectory execution metrics) into a **TimescaleDB** database, a PostgreSQL extension optimized for time-series data. The **Continuous Validation Service** cross-references executed trajectories (physical measurements) with simulated trajectories (digital twin predictions) by comparing distance metrics against predefined thresholds. Data integration is facilitated via an **MQTT Broker**, enabling asynchronous signaling between services. No explicit details on transmission frequencies, event triggers, or preprocessing steps (e.g., filtering) are provided in the document, though continuous logging implies real-time or near-real-time data flows. The system lacks explicit mention of data quality assurance mechanisms beyond the validation service’s threshold-based comparison.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the **Trajectory Generator Service**, which leverages a kinematics model to produce optimal movement trajectories for the crane, ensuring minimal residual swinging motion at trajectory endpoints. These commands are relayed through the **Control Application** (a Python service) to the **Physical Crane Interface**, which translates them into executable instructions for the crane’s controllers. The **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** allows operators to configure and override crane operations dynamically. The **Continuous Validation Service** indirectly validates control outcomes by comparing physical trajectory execution against simulated predictions, triggering alerts via the **Dashboard** if discrepancies exceed thresholds. No explicit details on command formats (e.g., message schemas), validation/authentication mechanisms (e.g., access control), or feedback systems (e.g., closed-loop adjustments) are provided. Command execution confirmation is implied through the validation service’s comparison process.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system employs an **MQTT Broker** for lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging between services, ensuring decoupled communication. Data storage relies on **TimescaleDB**, a PostgreSQL extension optimized for time-series workloads, housing all system-generated data. The **Dashboard** (Grafana) visualizes this data, while the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** interact via the MQTT Broker to exchange trajectory data. The **Database**, **MQTT Broker**, and **Dashboard** are containerized using **Docker**, with a provided `docker compose` file for orchestration. No explicit details on network topology (e.g., LAN/WAN), bandwidth/latency requirements, or security protocols (e.g., encryption) are documented. The system’s architecture suggests a localized deployment (e.g., on-premise) but does not specify redundancy or failover mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin system is deployed on a **personal computer**, with the physical crane interfacing directly via software running on the same machine. The **Database**, **MQTT Broker**, and **Dashboard** are containerized using **Docker**, while the remaining services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator**, **Simulation Service**) operate as standalone Python applications. A `docker compose` file is provided for managing containerized components. The system does not explicitly specify cloud/edge deployment options, computational/storage requirements (e.g., CPU/RAM), or scalability mechanisms. Availability/reliability assurances are implied by continuous logging and validation but not quantified. The deployment model prioritizes simplicity, with no mention of high-availability configurations or distributed architectures.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) employ **ad-hoc development approaches** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. Key methodologies include:
  - **Containerization/virtualization** for easy dissemination (e.g., virtual machine images with prerecorded data in GreenhouseDT, containerization in the gantry crane exemplar).
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** for the Air Quality Management exemplar, leveraging **AutomationML** for digital twin representation and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation.
  - **Self-adaptation** in GreenhouseDT, where both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin exhibit behavioral adaptation mechanisms.
  - **Single-board computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi)** for real-time sensor integration and data acquisition in the Air Quality Management exemplar.
  The absence of explicit lifecycle governance or transition management details (e.g., formal phases, version control, or evolution strategies) is noted, though **GitHub hosting** suggests collaborative development and version tracking via repositories (e.g., [GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT), [Air Quality Management](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system for the lab-scale gantry crane incorporates **runtime decision support** through a **Continuous Validation Service**, which monitors system performance and **notifies operators** of deviations or anomalies. A **Trajectory Generator Service** computes and optimizes movement trajectories for the crane's pucks (representing containers), ensuring efficient and collision-free operations. User interaction is facilitated via a **Human Machine Interface (HMI)**, enabling real-time visualization and control. The Air Quality Management exemplar provides **runtime visualization, simulation, and predictive analytics** for indoor air quality management, leveraging **AutomationML-based models** and **Microsoft Azure tools** for processing. The GreenhouseDT exemplar features **self-adaptive behavior** for both the physical greenhouse and its Digital Twin, though specific decision-making algorithms or confidence measures are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Air Quality Management exemplar adheres to:
  - **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, enabling **interoperability** with industrial automation tools.
  - **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, suggesting alignment with cloud-native standards (e.g., Azure IoT, Event Grid).
  GreenhouseDT uses **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker), which are de facto standards for IoT/data streaming but lack formal certification. The gantry crane exemplar’s architecture is documented via **C4 Model**, a software architecture standard, though no domain-specific standards (e.g., ISO 23227 for digital twins) are mentioned. **No certification requirements** or compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, IEC 62443) are referenced.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as a 'Lab-scale gantry crane' and its main components (Physical crane, Controller) based on the Abstract and Introduction. It correctly captures the operational domain (research, education) and key characteristics such as bi-directional interaction and open-source availability. However, the description includes speculative language ('likely a simplified or scaled-down version') not explicitly found in the text, which prefers direct statements like 'inspired by cranes in the harbor'. Quotes: 'in this exemplar paper we present a lab- scale gantry crane and its digital twin', 'The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller', 'bi-directional interaction', 'publicly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software'.

The extraction lists the correct services (Optimal control, Historical data logging, Data visualization, Continuous validation) as described in the Abstract and Section III.A. It provides specific capabilities for each service that align well with the text, such as generating optimal trajectories and comparing measurements with simulations. It also correctly notes the use for fault detection and DarTwin notation. Quotes: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations'.

The extraction correctly identifies key tools like CAD models, Kinematic Model, Python, Docker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, and Eclipse Mosquitto. It accurately describes their roles (e.g., TimescaleDB for time-series data, Grafana for visualization). However, it includes speculative examples not explicitly mentioned in the text for *this* exemplar (e.g., 'MATLAB, or ROS', 'SQL or NoSQL databases'—though TimescaleDB is SQL-based). The text explicitly states an ad-hoc approach without specific frameworks, which is correctly noted. Quotes: 'digital side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane', 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database', 'Dashboard: a Grafana dashboard', 'MQTT Broker... Eclipse Mosquitto', 'utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning:
The description accurately captures the data flow from the physical crane to the digital twin services, citing specific components like the Physical Crane Interface, Logging Application, and Continuous Validation Service. It correctly identifies the database technology (TimescaleDB) and the messaging protocol (MQTT). It also correctly notes the absence of specific transmission frequencies and preprocessing steps in the text.

Supporting quotes:
- "Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane."
- "Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database."
- "Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold."

The extraction precisely details the control flow, starting from the Trajectory Generator Service using a kinematics model, through the Control Application and Physical Crane Interface. It includes the role of the HMI and the validation feedback loop via the Dashboard. The acknowledgement of missing details (command formats, authentication) is consistent with the source content.

Supporting quotes:
- "Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no residual swinging motion at the end."
- "Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane."
- "Human Machine Interface: a Python front-end for the operator to configure and control the crane."

The characteristic is well-extracted, identifying MQTT as the communication protocol and explicitly naming the broker (Eclipse Mosquitto). It correctly describes the storage solution (TimescaleDB) and the visualization dashboard (Grafana). The mention of Docker containerization for specific components and the lack of network topology/security details aligns perfectly with the provided text.

Supporting quotes:
- "MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services."
- "Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used."
- "For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A docker compose file is provided..."

The description accurately summarizes the deployment strategy: running on a personal computer with a mix of Docker containers and a standalone Python module (`gantrylib`). It correctly identifies the specific components containerized versus those running as Python apps and references the docker compose file. The notes on the lack of cloud/edge options or high-availability details are accurate based on the source.

Supporting quotes:
- "plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer."
- "bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib."
- "For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker."

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
